content.full_text:
California 
Fair Political 
Practices Commission 
Vincent A. Muzzi 
1766 El Camino Real, suite C 
Burlingame, CA 
94010 
Dear Mr. Muzzi: 
October 4, 1985 
Re: 
Your Request for Assistance, 
Our File No. A-85-189 
This letter is sent in response to your request for advice 
and information on the Political Reform Act. 11 
You are 
considering running for the San Mateo County Board of 
Supervisors, and you are concerned about potential conflicts of 
interest based upon your ownership of land within the county. 
You are specifically concerned about decisions concerning the 
Planned Ag:r:icultural District ("PAD") in which you have direct 
and indirect interests in 500 acres of land. 
Since you are not 
yet a public official and thus there are no specific decisions 
pending, I cannot give you advice based on Section 83114(b). 
However, I hope the following general information and the 
enclosed materials will assist you in your decisions. 
As you apparently know, a public official is prohibited 
from making, participating in, or otherwise attempting to 
influence a governmental decision in which he has a financial 
interest in the outcome. 
section 87100. 
section 87103 
provides that an official has a financial interest in the 
outcome of a decision when it is reasonably foreseeable that 
the decision will have a material financial effect, 
distinguishable from its effects on the public generally, on 
any of the following: 
(a) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect investment worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
II The Political Reform Act is contained in Government 
Code sections 81000-91015. 
All statutory references are to the 
Government Code. 
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • 
(916)322-5660 


Mr. Vincent A. Muzzi 
October 4, 1985 
Page 2 
(b) 
Any real property in which the public 
official has a direct or indirect interest worth one 
thousand dollars ($1,000) or more. 
(c) 
Any source of income, other than gifts and 
other than loans by a commercial lending institution 
in the regular course of business on terms available 
to the public without regard to official status, 
aggregating two hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more 
in value provided to, received by or promised to the 
public official within 12 months prior to the time 
when the decision is made. 
(d) 
Any business entity in which the public 
official is a director, officer, partner, trustee, 
employee, or holds any position of management. 
(e) 
Any donor of, or any intermediary or agent 
for a donor of, a gift or gifts aggregating two 
hundred fifty dollars ($250) or more in value provided 
to, received by, or promised to the public official 
within 12 months prior to the time when the decision 
is made. 
section 87103. 
Accordingly, if you are elected to the Board of 
Supervisors, you should not participate in any decision which 
could have a material financial effect on the fair market value 
of your real property. 
Each decision must be analyzed based on 
all of the circumstances to determine whether an effect is 
reasonably foreseeable, whether the foreseeable effects are 
material, and whether the effects on your interests are 
distinguishable from the effects on the public generally or a 
significant segment thereof. 
You specifically raised questions concerning the 
application of the "public generally" language in section 
87103. 
At this point, I cannot tell you whether all of the 
County decisions concerning the PAD would fall within the 
"public generally" exception to disqualification. 
I would need 
more facts on the PAD, and I would need to know the precise 
decision before the county in order to analyze whether the 
effects of the decision on your property were the same as the 
effects on a significant segment of the property owners in the 
county. 
I enclose a copy of the Commission's recent opinion 
involving Santa Clara County Supervisor Thomas Legan; the 


Mr. Vincent A. Muzzi 
October 4, 1985 
Page 3 
analysis in the opinion is illustrative of the type of analysis 
which would be applied to your situation. 
I also enclose 
copies of relevant advice letters and regulations. 
Please feel free to contact me again if I can be of further 
assistance. 
Enclosures 
Sincerely, 
lUAU 71i}.)(,1{jlJU;e,lJu1 tL 
Diane Maura Fishburn 
/VV?) ) 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


Ms. Barbara Milman 
Vincent A. M uzzi 
and Counselor at La)D p. 
j 
1766 El Camino Real, SUit~tt 
Burlingame, California 94010 
28, 1985 
Political Practices Commission 
Legal Staff 
P.O. Box 807 
Sacramento, Ca. 95804 
Re: Con 
ict 0 
Interest Advice 
Dear Ms. 
lman: 
Telephone 
415-692-5406 
It is my understanding that the Fair Political Practices 
Commission is authorized by law to provide advice letters and 
opinions on 
whether 
conflicts of interest exist under the 
Political Reform 
Act 
of 
1974 
as 
amended 
I am presently 
evaluating whether 
I 
will 
run 
for 
Supervisor within the 
County of San 
Mateo 
and as a initial matter wish to receive 
your advice 
concerning 
the impact of my real property hold-
ings within 
County 
on 
my 
ability to participate in 
certain 
Board decisions. 
I 
have 
received conflicting 
opinion about the present 
status of California law in this 
area and request your clarifications. 
If I decide to run for 
office, I expect that a 
major 
issue 
in my campaign will be 
the 
Planned Agricultural District 
(PAD) 
zoning 
and 
the 
uses allowed thereunder. 
Specifically, 
I 
own 
400 
acre 
m.o.l. 
unincorporated San 
Mateo County. 
All of that land is 
the 
P 
Agricultural 
District 
(PAD) . 
The 
unincorporated 
area 
i 
ly 140,000 acres of 
within 
within 
total 
5,000 
the PAD zoning. 
Pres 
100 
the

that 
landowners 
would 
constitute a "significant segment of 
the public" 
with 
the meaning of the law. 
Further, it is my 
understanding 
that if 
a 
Board action would impact 
land 
dif 
than others owning real property within the PAD 
that 
I 
would 
be required to disqualify myself. 
Although I 
would 
anticipate 
my 
disqualification if a relative's land 
were 
affected differently than other landholders, it is my 
understanding that 
the 
law itself does 
not require such 
squali 
ion. 
What perhaps 
is most 
important is a correction of the 
assumption that some 
people 
have 
made that I would be dis-
quali 
from 
consideration of 
actions affecting the PAD 
simply because I own land within that district. 
I hope that 
I 
have explained my concerns and the issue 
clearly. 
If you 
require additional information, I would be 
happy to provide it to you or your staff. 
I would appreciate 
an early written response. 
Thank you for your attention to this matter. 
Sincerely, 
Vincent A Muzzi

sections.question: You are considering running for the San Mateo County Board of Supervisors, and you are concerned about potential conflicts of interest based upon your ownership of land within the county. You are specifically concerned about decisions concerning the Planned Agricultural District ("PAD") in which you have direct and indirect interests in 500 acres of land.
sections.conclusion: Since you are not yet a public official and thus there are no specific decisions pending, I cannot give you advice based on Section 83114(b). However, if you are elected to the Board of Supervisors, you should not participate in any decision which could have a material financial effect on the fair market value of your real property. Each decision must be analyzed based on all of the circumstances to determine whether an effect is reasonably foreseeable, whether the foreseeable effects are material, and whether the effects on your interests are distinguishable from the effects on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. At this point, I cannot tell you whether all of the County decisions concerning the PAD would fall within the "public generally" exception to disqualification without more facts on the PAD and knowledge of the precise decision pending before the county.

in the opinion is illustrative of the type of analysis 
which would be applied to your situation. 
I also enclose 
copies of relevant advice letters and regulations.
sections.question_synthetic: Whether the requestor's ownership of approximately 500 acres within a Planned Agricultural District would create disqualifying conflicts of interest if elected to the San Mateo County Board of Supervisors, and whether county decisions affecting the PAD would fall within the "public generally" exception to the conflict of interest rules.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC cannot provide specific advice since no decisions are pending, but advises that if elected, the requestor must disqualify from decisions with material financial effects on his real property, with each decision requiring case-by-case analysis of whether effects on his property differ from effects on the public generally or a significant segment thereof.
sections.parsing_notes: Format: ocr; LLM: Document includes both incoming request letter (from Muzzi, dated September 28, 1985) and FPPC response letter (dated October 4, 1985). Main response letter is the FPPC advice. OCR quality is generally good with minor artifacts. Requestor owns 400 acres per his letter, FPPC references 500 acres in response. The advice is general and hypothetical since no specific pending decisions existed at time of inquiry.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: You are considering running for the San Mateo County Board of Supervisors, and you are concerned about potential conflicts of interest based upon your ownership of land within the county. You are specifically concerned about decisions concerning the Planned Agricultural District ("PAD") in which you have direct and indirect interests in 500 acres of land.

CONCLUSION: Since you are not yet a public official and thus there are no specific decisions pending, I cannot give you advice based on Section 83114(b). However, if you are elected to the Board of Supervisors, you should not participate in any decision which could have a material financial effect on the fair market value of your real property. Each decision must be analyzed based on all of the circumstances to determine whether an effect is reasonably foreseeable, whether the foreseeable effects are material, and whether the effects on your interests are distinguishable from the effects on the public generally or a significant segment thereof. At this point, I cannot tell you whether all of the County decisions concerning the PAD would fall within the "public generally" exception to disqualification without more facts on the PAD and knowledge of the precise decision pending before the county.

embedding.summary: FPPC response to Vincent A. Muzzi's request for advice regarding potential conflicts of interest arising from his ownership of approximately 500 acres within a Planned Agricultural District if he runs for San Mateo County Board of Supervisors. The FPPC provides general guidance on Section 87100-87103 conflict of interest rules but cannot give specific advice without pending decisions being identified.
